skill.name: code-instructor
skill.description: Educational coding skill that teaches programming concepts while building working applications. It explains code step by step, adapts to learner skill level, highlights common mistakes, and can use a bundled annotator script to create teaching-focused comments.
skill.summary: Teach programming through guided, incremental app development.
skill.author: Emz1998

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command and critical heuristic hits, but review found these are Markdown code fences, inline code examples, and educational snippets rather than executable shell or Ruby backticks. The only confirmed behavioral risk is a bundled Python annotator script that reads a user-specified file and writes an annotated copy, so the skill is low risk with a filesystem warning.

low_findings.title: Bundled Script Writes Annotated Files
low_findings.description: The annotator script accepts a user-provided file path, reads that file, and writes a derived annotated output path. This is legitimate for a teaching helper, but it can create or overwrite local files if run on sensitive paths without care.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file read and write behavior is explicit in the script and the skill documents how to run it. The behavior is local and user-invoked, so the severity remains low.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The many reported Ruby or shell backtick findings occur in Markdown code fences and inline code examples used for programming instruction. I did not find evidence that these examples execute commands, exfiltrate data, or override the assistant instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are visibly Markdown examples and ordinary code snippets. No semantic evidence of shell execution exists at these locations.

low_findings.title: Network and Sensitive Data Findings Are Educational Examples
low_findings.description: The fetch call and SQLite connection are illustrative beginner mistakes in reference material. The fetch uses a placeholder URL variable, and the SQLite line appears inside an architecture example, not an operational data access flow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both patterns are embedded in sections labeled as mistakes and teaching responses. No external endpoint, credentials, or real database file access path is present beyond the example text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Read and Write in Helper Script
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper script reads a path from command-line input and writes an annotated sibling file. Users should run it only on intended source files and review the generated output path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly performs local filesystem operations from user-supplied arguments. There is no network or credential access tied to this behavior.

content.value_statement: Learning code can feel abstract when explanations are separated from real projects. This skill teaches concepts while building working apps step by step.
actual_capabilities: Breaks application builds into small, teachable phases.
Explains the purpose and execution flow of code blocks.
Adapts explanations for beginner, intermediate, and advanced learners.
Uses references for common beginner mistakes and teaching patterns.
Provides a Python script that inserts educational comment markers into source files.
limitations: The annotator script adds templates, not complete human-quality explanations.
It focuses on Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, and React examples.
It does not verify that generated applications are production ready.
It may produce lengthy explanations for users who only want concise code.

use_cases.title: Build a First App
use_cases.description: Create a small working app while learning each concept in sequence.

use_cases.title: Understand Existing Code
use_cases.description: Turn unfamiliar code into a guided lesson with comments and execution flow.

use_cases.title: Prepare Teaching Material
use_cases.description: Structure examples, corrections, and common mistake explanations for a coding lesson.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Beginner Project
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner wants to learn through a small app.
prompt_templates.prompt: Teach me by building a simple todo app. Explain each file, each major line, and why each step matters.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Code Block
prompt_templates.scenario: Learner has code they do not understand.
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain this code like a lesson. Cover the purpose, prerequisites, execution flow, common mistakes, and a small practice change.

prompt_templates.title: Debug as a Lesson
prompt_templates.scenario: Learner has an error and needs the concept behind the fix.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me debug this error. Show what went wrong, why it happened, the fix, and the general rule I should remember.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Advanced Learning Path
prompt_templates.scenario: Experienced learner wants deeper architecture guidance.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build this feature in phases from simple to production-minded. Explain trade-offs, testing points, architecture choices, and refactoring moments.

output_examples.input: I want to build a todo app and learn React state.
output_examples.output: A phased lesson plan starts with static todos, then adds state, form input, completion toggles, deletion, and browser storage. Each phase includes concise explanations and checks for understanding.

output_examples.input: Why is my async JavaScript returning undefined?
output_examples.output: The response explains timing, shows why the value is read too early, introduces await or then handling, and gives a short practice change.

output_examples.input: Make this file easier for students to study.
output_examples.output: The skill can suggest running the annotator script and then reviewing inserted purpose, parameter, return, loop, and condition notes.

best_practices: Ask for the learner skill level before choosing explanation depth.
Build the smallest working version before adding polish or architecture.
Run or review each major step before moving to the next concept.
anti_patterns: Do not request a complete large app without intermediate learning checkpoints.
Do not use the annotator script on files you do not intend to copy or annotate.
Do not skip the why behind a fix when the goal is learning.

faq.question: Can this skill write complete applications?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed to build apps incrementally while explaining each important decision.

faq.question: Is it only for beginners?
faq.answer: No. It can adjust from syntax-level explanations to architecture and trade-off discussions.

faq.question: Which languages does it emphasize?
faq.answer: The references emphasize Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, and React teaching patterns.

faq.question: Does the script change my original file?
faq.answer: The script reads the original file and writes a separate annotated file path derived from the name.

faq.question: Can it help with debugging?
faq.answer: Yes. It frames errors as lessons by explaining the cause, fix, and reusable rule.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: The audit found low risk. The main caution is the local filesystem behavior of the optional annotator script.
